name: Melatonin
description: A hormone that regulates sleep-wake cycles. Available as an over-the-counter supplement.
shortDescription: Natural hormone to support sleep onset
safetyNotes: Start with lowest dose (0.5-1mg). Don't use with alcohol. Consult doctor if taking blood thinners or immunosuppressants.
commonMistakes: Taking too close to bedtime (take 30-60 min before)
instructions: Take 0.5-1mg about 30-60 minutes before your target bedtime. Start low and only increase if needed.
quickProtocol: Take 0.5mg melatonin with a small sip of water. Turn off screens. Dim lights for 20 minutes.

name: Magnesium Glycinate
description: A highly absorbable form of magnesium that may help with relaxation and sleep quality.
shortDescription: Mineral for muscle relaxation and calm
safetyNotes: Consult doctor if you have kidney disease. May interact with some antibiotics and blood pressure medications.
commonMistakes: Taking on empty stomach (causes stomach upset)
Using wrong form (glycinate is best for sleep)
Taking too late (can cause morning grogginess)
instructions: Take 200-400mg of magnesium glycinate 30-60 minutes before bed. Best absorbed with food.
quickProtocol: Take 200mg magnesium glycinate with dinner or a light snack.

name: L-Theanine
description: An amino acid found in tea that promotes relaxation without drowsiness.
shortDescription: Calming amino acid from green tea
safetyNotes: Generally very safe. May lower blood pressure - consult doctor if on blood pressure medications.
commonMistakes: Taking too high dose (can cause vivid dreams)
Not giving it time to work
instructions: Take 100-200mg of L-theanine 30 minutes before bed. Can be combined with magnesium.
quickProtocol: Take 100mg L-theanine. Practice 4-7-8 breathing for 5 minutes.

name: 4-7-8 Breathing Technique
description: A breathing pattern that activates the parasympathetic nervous system to induce calm.
safetyNotes: Safe for most people. If you feel dizzy, return to normal breathing.
commonMistakes: Getting frustrated after a few attempts
instructions: Inhale through nose for 4 seconds, hold for 7 seconds, exhale through mouth for 8 seconds. Repeat 4 times.
quickProtocol: Inhale 4 counts, hold 7 counts, exhale 8 counts. Repeat 4 times. Focus on the exhale.

name: Cognitive Unload / Journaling
description: Writing down thoughts and worries to get them out of your head before sleep.
safetyNotes: No risks. Keep a notebook by your bed.
instructions: Write for 5-10 minutes about: what's on your mind, what you're worried about, what you need to remember tomorrow. Don't edit or solve - just dump.
quickProtocol: Write 3 things: 1) What's worrying me, 2) What I need to do tomorrow, 3) One thing I'm grateful for. 5 minutes max.

name: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
description: Systematically tensing and releasing muscle groups to reduce physical tension.
shortDescription: Release body tension step by step
safetyNotes: May not be suitable if you have muscle injuries. Go gently on sore areas.
instructions: Starting from your toes: tense each muscle group for 5 seconds, then release. Work up through your body: feet, legs, abdomen, hands, arms, shoulders, face.
quickProtocol: Tense and release: feet (5 sec), then legs, then stomach, then hands, then shoulders. Breathe out on release.

name: White Noise / Pink Noise
description: Consistent background sound that masks environmental noises and promotes sleep continuity.
safetyNotes: Keep volume low (below 50 dB). Don't use earbuds while sleeping.
commonMistakes: Having it on all night when not needed
instructions: Set to a comfortable volume that masks outside noise but isn't distracting. Use a timer if you only need it to fall asleep.
quickProtocol: Put on white noise or nature sounds at low volume. Focus on the sound, letting it fill your attention.

name: Stimulus Control
description: Bedroom re-association technique to strengthen the bed-sleep connection.
shortDescription: Train your brain to associate bed with sleep
safetyNotes: If awake in bed for 20 minutes, get up. This is not harmful - it's therapeutic.
instructions: Rule: If you're awake in bed for more than 20 minutes, get up, go to another room, do something boring until sleepy, then return to bed. Only use bed for sleep and intimacy.
quickProtocol: If awake >20 min: get up, dim lights, read something boring or listen to calm audio, return when drowsy.

name: Chamomile Tea
description: Herbal tea with mild sedative properties that can aid relaxation before bed.
safetyNotes: Avoid if allergic to ragweed, chrysanthemums, or marigolds. Not for pregnancy.
commonMistakes: Drinking too close to bedtime (need to use bathroom)
instructions: Drink 1-2 cups about 90 minutes before bed. Use water that's not too hot. Add nothing - just chamomile.
quickProtocol: Steep chamomile tea for 5 min. Sip slowly while doing 4-7-8 breathing.

name: NSDR (Yoga Nidra)
description: Non-Sleep Deep Rest - a guided body scan that promotes deep relaxation.
shortDescription: Guided body scan for deep relaxation
safetyNotes: Don't use while driving. Some people find body scans triggering if they have trauma history.
instructions: Lie flat on your back. Follow a 20-30 minute guided NSDR script. Focus on body sensations and breath. Allow yourself to be guided.
quickProtocol: Lie down, close eyes, follow a 10-minute body scan meditation. Let your body sink into the floor.

name: Screen Time Reduction
description: Reducing blue light exposure before bed to support natural melatonin production.
safetyNotes: Use night mode on devices if reduction isn't possible.
instructions: Stop all screens 60 minutes before bed. If impossible, use blue light blocking glasses and night mode on all devices.
quickProtocol: Put phone in another room or on airplane mode. Use lamps instead of overhead lights.

name: Bedtime Wind-Down Routine
description: A consistent 30-60 minute pre-sleep routine to signal your body it's time for rest.
safetyNotes: Be consistent with timing. The routine itself matters more than specific activities.
instructions: Create a 30-60 minute routine: dim lights, reduce noise, light stretching or meditation, maybe herbal tea or reading. Do it at the same time every night.
quickProtocol: 30 min before bed: lights dim, screen off, gentle stretch, journal, chamomile tea.
